strawberry.wateringNotes: Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. Water at base to avoid fruit rot.
strawberry.feedingSchedule: High potash liquid feed fortnightly once flowering begins

commonProblems.symptoms: Fuzzy grey mould on fruit, soft rotting berries
commonProblems.cause: Damp conditions, poor air circulation, fruit touching soil
commonProblems.treatment: Remove affected fruit. Straw mulch under plants. Improve airflow between plants.

commonProblems.symptoms: Holes eaten in fruit, slime trails
commonProblems.treatment: Beer traps, copper tape around pots, straw mulch, evening patrols.

commonProblems.symptoms: White powdery patches on leaves
commonProblems.treatment: Water regularly at base. Remove worst-affected leaves. Improve spacing.

strawberry.harvestTips: Pick when fully red all over. Best picked in morning when cool. Eat same day for best flavour.
strawberry.repottingTrigger: When roots fill the pot or plant produces fewer runners
strawberry.repottingSize: Final pot 25-30cm diameter, or plant out in beds

monthlyTasks.01: Order new plants if needed. Check stored runners for signs of life.
monthlyTasks.02: Sow seeds indoors on surface of moist compost — don't cover, they need light to germinate.
monthlyTasks.03: Keep seedlings at 15-18°C. Pot on when first true leaves appear. Start hardening off late month.
monthlyTasks.04: Plant out hardened-off plants. Protect from late frost with fleece. Mulch with straw.
monthlyTasks.05: Water regularly as flowers appear. Start liquid feeding with tomato feed. Watch for slugs.
monthlyTasks.06: Peak strawberry season! Pick daily. Remove runners unless you want new plants. Keep watering.
monthlyTasks.07: Continue picking. Feed fortnightly. Peg down runners you want to keep into small pots.
monthlyTasks.08: Harvest slows. Remove old straw mulch. Cut back tatty foliage on summer-fruiting types.
monthlyTasks.09: Plant new runners. Tidy plants. Reduce watering as growth slows.
monthlyTasks.10: Clear away old plants over 3 years old. Protect late crops from frost.
monthlyTasks.11: Mulch crowns with straw for winter protection.
monthlyTasks.12: Rest. Plan varieties for next year. Order bare-root runners.

sweet-pepper-salad-festival.wateringNotes: Water consistently — irregular watering causes blossom end rot. Let top inch dry between waterings.
sweet-pepper-salad-festival.feedingSchedule: Tomato feed fortnightly once first fruits set
sweet-pepper-salad-festival.supportType: Short cane or stake — plants get top-heavy when fruiting

commonProblems.symptoms: Dark sunken patch at bottom of fruit
commonProblems.treatment: Water more consistently. Mulch to retain moisture. Don't over-feed with nitrogen.

commonProblems.symptoms: Clusters of small green/black insects on shoot tips, sticky leaves
commonProblems.treatment: Blast off with water spray. Introduce ladybirds. Pinch off heavily infested tips.

sweet-pepper-salad-festival.harvestTips: Pick green for mild flavour, leave to ripen red/yellow for sweeter taste. Cut with secateurs, don't pull.
sweet-pepper-salad-festival.repottingTrigger: Pot on when roots emerge from drainage holes, typically 3 times before final pot
sweet-pepper-salad-festival.repottingSize: Final pot 25-30cm, or grow bags (2 per bag)

monthlyTasks.01: Order seeds. Clean pots and seed trays.
monthlyTasks.02: Sow indoors at 21-25°C. Use a heated propagator if possible. Bottom heat really helps.
monthlyTasks.03: Pot on seedlings into 9cm pots. Keep warm — peppers hate cold. Don't rush them.
monthlyTasks.04: Pot on again to 2L pots. Keep indoors. Start hardening off at end of month if warm.
monthlyTasks.05: Plant out after last frost ONLY if night temps reliably above 12°C. Greenhouse is safer in UK.
monthlyTasks.06: Stake plants. Pinch out growing tip at 30cm to encourage bushing. Start liquid feeding when flowers appear.
monthlyTasks.07: Water and feed regularly. Pick first fruits green to encourage more.
monthlyTasks.08: Peak harvest. Keep picking. Support laden branches.
monthlyTasks.09: Last harvests. Strip remaining fruits before first frost — they'll ripen on a windowsill.
monthlyTasks.10: Clear plants. Compost healthy material. Save seeds from best fruits if open-pollinated.
monthlyTasks.12: Plan next year. Consider different varieties.

onion-ailsa-craig.wateringNotes: Water during dry spells. Stop watering completely once foliage starts yellowing — the bulbs need to dry out for storage.
onion-ailsa-craig.feedingSchedule: General purpose liquid feed monthly until June, then stop

commonProblems.symptoms: Yellowing, drooping foliage. Maggots in bulb.
commonProblems.cause: Flies laying eggs near base of plants
commonProblems.treatment: Cover with enviromesh from May. Firm soil around base. Avoid thinning which releases scent.

commonProblems.symptoms: Yellow leaves, white fluffy fungus at base, bulb rots
commonProblems.treatment: No cure — remove and destroy affected plants. Don't grow alliums in same spot again.

commonProblems.symptoms: Flower stalk forms from centre of bulb
commonProblems.cause: Cold snap after warm period, or planting too early
commonProblems.treatment: Snap off flower stalk immediately. Use bolt-resistant varieties. Don't sow too early.

onion-ailsa-craig.harvestTips: Harvest when foliage yellows and flops over naturally. Lay on surface to dry for 2 weeks. Store in cool, dry, airy place — plaited strings or mesh bags.
onion-ailsa-craig.repottingTrigger: Pot on from modules to individual 9cm pots when 10-15cm tall
onion-ailsa-craig.repottingSize: 9cm pots, then plant out to final position

monthlyTasks.01: Sow Ailsa Craig indoors in modules. Keep at 10-15°C. They're tough and germinate easily.
monthlyTasks.02: Pot on seedlings when pencil-thick. Keep growing steadily. Don't let them get leggy.
monthlyTasks.03: Continue potting on. Harden off at end of month. Sow direct outdoors if ground is workable.
monthlyTasks.04: Plant out into final positions. Firm in well — onions like firm soil. Space 10cm apart.
monthlyTasks.05: Keep weed-free — onions hate competition. Water in dry spells. Don't over-feed.
monthlyTasks.06: Stop feeding. Keep weeding. Water if very dry. Bulbs should be swelling now.
monthlyTasks.07: Foliage starts to yellow — stop watering completely. Bend over remaining foliage to encourage drying.
monthlyTasks.08: Harvest when foliage is papery. Lay out to dry in sun for 2 weeks. Move under cover if rain threatens.
monthlyTasks.09: Finish drying and store. Plant autumn-sown onion sets for next year.
monthlyTasks.10: Enjoy stored onions. Check for any going soft and use those first.
monthlyTasks.12: Order seed for next year. Ailsa Craig is an exhibition variety — grows huge!

sweet-pea.wateringNotes: Water deeply at base. Keep soil consistently moist but not soggy. Dry roots cause bud drop.
sweet-pea.feedingSchedule: Fortnightly high-potash liquid feed once flowering begins
sweet-pea.supportType: Canes, netting, or trellis — they climb to 2m via tendrils
sweet-pea.pinchOutAt: 10cm height — forces bushy growth from base, many more stems

commonProblems.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves, usually late season
commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation, dry roots with humid air
commonProblems.treatment: Improve airflow. Water at base regularly. Remove worst-affected leaves.

commonProblems.symptoms: Flower buds fall off before opening
commonProblems.cause: Irregular watering, extreme heat, or not picking enough
commonProblems.treatment: Keep soil consistently moist. Mulch around base. Pick flowers every few days.

commonProblems.symptoms: Tall, thin, weak stems
commonProblems.treatment: Move to brighter spot. Keep cooler (10-15°C). Pinch out tips at 10cm.

sweet-pea.harvestTips: Pick flowers regularly — every 2-3 days — to encourage more blooms. Cut stems long for vases. If you stop picking, they set seed and stop flowering.
sweet-pea.repottingTrigger: Roots visible at drainage holes, plant becomes top-heavy
sweet-pea.repottingSize: Move from 9cm to 2L pot, then plant out in final position

monthlyTasks.01: Sow seeds indoors in root trainers or toilet roll tubes. Nick seed coat or soak overnight for faster germination.
monthlyTasks.02: Continue indoor sowing. Keep at 10-15°C — they prefer cool. Don't overwater.
monthlyTasks.03: Pinch out tips when 10cm tall. This is the single most important thing you can do. Harden off late-sown plants.
monthlyTasks.04: Plant out hardened-off seedlings after last frost risk. Install supports first. Water in well.
monthlyTasks.05: Tie in to supports as they grow — loosely, they're delicate. Start liquid feeding when buds form.
monthlyTasks.06: Deadhead spent flowers ruthlessly. Pick regularly for more blooms. Water deeply in dry spells.
monthlyTasks.07: Peak flowering! Keep picking every few days. Feed fortnightly with high-potash feed.
monthlyTasks.08: Continue picking. Watch for mildew in dry spells. Water at base, not over foliage.
monthlyTasks.09: Flowering slows. Collect seeds from best plants for next year. Let a few pods dry on the vine.
monthlyTasks.10: Pull out spent plants. Autumn sow in cold frame for earlier flowers next year.
monthlyTasks.11: Autumn-sown seedlings: protect with cloche or cold frame. They'll be tough little plants by spring.
monthlyTasks.12: Rest. Plan next year's varieties. Sweet peas make incredible cut flowers.

sunflower.wateringNotes: Deep watering once or twice a week. They're surprisingly drought tolerant once established but perform best with regular water.
sunflower.feedingSchedule: General purpose feed monthly. Switch to high-potash when buds form.
sunflower.supportType: Sturdy cane or stake for tall varieties — they can reach 3m and catch wind

commonProblems.symptoms: Seedlings eaten overnight, slime trails
commonProblems.treatment: Start indoors. When planting out, use copper rings, beer traps, or nematodes.

commonProblems.symptoms: Stem snaps or plant topples in wind
commonProblems.cause: Top-heavy flower head, exposed position, inadequate support
commonProblems.treatment: Stake early while stem is still growing. Position in sheltered spot if possible.

sunflower.harvestTips: For seeds: leave flower head on plant until back turns brown and seeds darken. Cut with 30cm stem, hang upside down in dry place. For birds: just leave them standing!
sunflower.repottingTrigger: When roots fill the pot — sunflowers grow fast, don't let them get root-bound or they stunt
sunflower.repottingSize: Move quickly from modules to 2L pots, then plant out ASAP — they don't like being pot-bound

monthlyTasks.01: Plan varieties. Giant? Multi-headed? Dwarf for pots?
monthlyTasks.02: Order seeds. Prepare growing position — dig in compost.
monthlyTasks.03: Sow indoors from mid-month in 9cm pots. One seed per pot, 2cm deep.
monthlyTasks.04: Pot on if needed. Harden off. Sow direct outdoors from mid-month if soil is warm.
monthlyTasks.05: Plant out hardened-off seedlings. Stake immediately. Water in well. Protect from slugs.
monthlyTasks.06: Growing fast now! Water deeply once a week. Feed monthly. Tie to stake as they grow.
monthlyTasks.07: Buds forming. Switch to high-potash feed. Keep watering. They can drink a LOT.
monthlyTasks.08: Flowers open! Enjoy. Photograph. The bees will love them. Keep watering and feeding.
monthlyTasks.09: Flowers fading. Leave seed heads for birds or harvest seeds.
monthlyTasks.10: Cut down spent stems. Compost them. Save seeds if wanted.
monthlyTasks.12: Plan next year. Maybe try a sunflower height competition?

tomato.wateringNotes: Water consistently and deeply. Irregular watering causes blossom end rot and split fruit. Water at base, never over foliage.
tomato.feedingSchedule: High-potash tomato feed twice weekly once first truss sets fruit
tomato.supportType: Cane, stake, or string support. Cordon types need single stem training. Bush types can be left.

commonProblems.symptoms: Dark, sunken, leathery patch at bottom of fruit
commonProblems.cause: Calcium deficiency caused by irregular watering
commonProblems.treatment: Water consistently. Don't let soil dry out between waterings. Mulch to retain moisture.

commonProblems.symptoms: Brown patches on leaves and stems, fruit turns brown and rots
commonProblems.cause: Phytophthora infestans fungus — spreads in warm, wet weather
commonProblems.treatment: Remove affected parts immediately. Grow under cover where possible. Choose blight-resistant varieties.

commonProblems.symptoms: Cracks in skin of ripening fruit
commonProblems.cause: Sudden heavy watering after dry period
commonProblems.treatment: Water consistently. Pick fruit as soon as it cracks — still edible.

tomato.harvestTips: Pick when fully coloured. If frost threatens, pick green — they'll ripen on a sunny windowsill or in a drawer with a banana.
tomato.repottingTrigger: Pot on as soon as roots fill current pot — tomatoes are hungry, fast growers
tomato.repottingSize: 9cm → 2L → final 10L pot or grow bag (2-3 per bag)

monthlyTasks.01: Order seeds. Clean pots and greenhouse.
monthlyTasks.02: Sow indoors from mid-month at 20-25°C. Heated propagator ideal. Don't sow too early — leggy, stressed plants.
monthlyTasks.03: Pot on into 9cm pots. Grow on at 15-18°C. Good light essential — windowsill or grow light.
monthlyTasks.04: Pot on to 2L. Start hardening off end of month. Still too cold to plant out!
monthlyTasks.05: Plant out after last frost — mid-May at earliest in most of UK. Support immediately.
monthlyTasks.06: Pinch out side shoots on cordon types (every few days — they grow fast!). Start feeding when first truss sets.
monthlyTasks.07: Feed twice weekly. Water deeply daily in hot weather. Continue removing side shoots. First fruits ripening!
monthlyTasks.08: Peak harvest! Keep feeding and watering. Remove lower leaves below ripening trusses for air circulation.
monthlyTasks.09: Last outdoor harvests. Pick green before frost — ripen indoors. Remove plant tops to focus energy on remaining fruit.
monthlyTasks.10: Clear spent plants. Green tomatoes → chutney. Clean greenhouse.

basil.wateringNotes: Water in the morning. Let soil surface dry slightly between waterings. Hates cold, wet roots — will sulk and rot.

commonProblems.symptoms: Flower spikes appear, leaves become bitter
commonProblems.cause: Heat stress, not picking regularly enough
commonProblems.treatment: Pinch out flower buds immediately. Pick leaves regularly from the top. Successive sow for continuous supply.

commonProblems.symptoms: Seedlings collapse at soil level, stems go brown
commonProblems.treatment: Use clean compost. Don't overwater. Good ventilation. Keep warm (18°C+).

basil.harvestTips: Always pick from the top, pinching above a leaf pair. This encourages bushy growth. Never strip all leaves from one plant.
basil.repottingTrigger: When seedlings have 2-3 pairs of true leaves
basil.repottingSize: 2-3 seedlings per 15cm pot, or plant out in groups

monthlyTasks.02: Still too early for most — basil needs warmth.
monthlyTasks.03: Sow indoors on windowsill at 20°C+. Surface sow — seeds need light. Keep warm!
monthlyTasks.04: Pot on seedlings. Sow more for succession. Keep indoors — still too cold out.
monthlyTasks.05: Plant out only if reliably warm (18°C+). Safer on a sunny windowsill in UK. Sow more.
monthlyTasks.06: Start harvesting by pinching tips. This makes plants bushy. Water and feed regularly.
monthlyTasks.07: Peak harvest. Pinch out any flower buds. Sow another batch for autumn supply.
monthlyTasks.08: Continue harvesting. Make pesto with gluts! Plants may start to tire.
monthlyTasks.09: Last harvests. Bring pots indoors before first frost. Basil dies at first hint of cold.
monthlyTasks.10: Clear outdoor plants. Indoor pots may continue on sunny windowsill.
monthlyTasks.11: Rest. Indoor basil struggles with low light — consider grow lights.
monthlyTasks.12: Plan next year. Try different varieties — Thai basil, purple basil, lemon basil.

sweet-pepper-unwins.wateringNotes: Consistent watering is key. Irregular watering causes blossom end rot.
sweet-pepper-unwins.feedingSchedule: Tomato feed fortnightly once first fruits set
sweet-pepper-unwins.supportType: Short cane — plants get top-heavy with fruit

commonProblems.symptoms: Dark sunken patch at bottom of fruit
commonProblems.treatment: Water more consistently. Mulch to retain moisture.

commonProblems.symptoms: Clusters of insects on new growth, sticky leaves
commonProblems.treatment: Blast with water. Encourage ladybirds. Pinch off worst-affected tips.

sweet-pepper-unwins.harvestTips: Pick green or leave to ripen to full colour. Cut don't pull. Picking regularly encourages more fruit.
sweet-pepper-unwins.repottingTrigger: Pot on when roots show at drainage holes

monthlyTasks.02: Sow indoors at 21-25°C. Heated propagator essential.
monthlyTasks.04: Pot on again. Start hardening off end of month if warm.
monthlyTasks.05: Plant out after last frost. Greenhouse preferred in UK.
monthlyTasks.06: Stake. Pinch growing tip at 30cm. Start feeding when flowers appear.
monthlyTasks.07: Water and feed. Pick first fruits green.
monthlyTasks.09: Last harvests before frost. Green fruit ripens on windowsill.

poppy-mixed.wateringNotes: Very drought tolerant once established. Overwatering is the main killer. Only water in prolonged dry spells.
poppy-mixed.feedingSchedule: None needed — poppies prefer poor soil

commonProblems.symptoms: Seedlings collapse
commonProblems.treatment: Sow thinly. Don't overwater. Good drainage essential.

commonProblems.symptoms: Clusters on stems and buds
commonProblems.treatment: Blast off with water. Let natural predators handle it.

poppy-mixed.harvestTips: Let seed pods dry on the plant for self-seeding next year. Shake pods over border for naturalising.

monthlyTasks.02: Prepare ground — rake to fine tilth.
monthlyTasks.03: Scatter seed on surface. Don't cover — they need light. Water gently. They'll self-sow once established.
monthlyTasks.04: Thin seedlings to 15-30cm apart. Don't transplant — poppies hate root disturbance.
monthlyTasks.05: Leave them alone! They don't need feeding or much water.
monthlyTasks.06: Flowers appear! Enjoy the show. Don't deadhead if you want self-seeding.
monthlyTasks.07: Continue flowering. Seed pods forming on spent flowers.
monthlyTasks.08: Last flowers. Pods drying. Shake pods where you want poppies next year.
monthlyTasks.09: Clear spent plants or leave pods for birds and self-seeding.
monthlyTasks.10: Autumn sow for earlier flowers next year — just scatter on bare ground.

poppy-scarlet.wateringNotes: Drought tolerant. Less is more with poppies.
poppy-scarlet.feedingSchedule: None — poppies thrive in poor soil

commonProblems.symptoms: Seedlings collapse at soil level
commonProblems.treatment: Sow thinly. Good drainage. Don't overwater.

poppy-scarlet.harvestTips: Classic field poppy — let it self-seed for naturalised drifts of scarlet.

monthlyTasks.03: Scatter seed. Don't cover. Water lightly.
monthlyTasks.06: Stunning scarlet flowers appear! The bees and butterflies will flock.
monthlyTasks.08: Last flowers. Let pods dry for self-seeding.
monthlyTasks.10: Autumn sow for earlier display next year.

foxglove.wateringNotes: Keep moist but not waterlogged. They're woodland plants — they like dappled shade and moist soil.
foxglove.feedingSchedule: Light feed in spring when growth resumes

commonProblems.symptoms: Plant collapses, base is mushy
commonProblems.treatment: Improve drainage. Don't plant in heavy clay without amending. Raise soil level.

commonProblems.symptoms: Clusters on flower spikes
commonProblems.treatment: Blast off with water. They rarely cause serious harm to foxgloves.

foxglove.harvestTips: Not edible — foxgloves are toxic. All parts are poisonous. Wear gloves when handling. Beautiful but deadly!
foxglove.repottingTrigger: Pot on when rosette fills current pot
foxglove.repottingSize: 2L pot, then plant out in autumn for flowering the following year

monthlyTasks.01: Check overwintering plants. Remove dead leaves.
monthlyTasks.02: Plants start putting on new growth. Light feed.
monthlyTasks.03: Sow indoors if wanted. Surface sow — tiny seeds need light. Existing plants growing strongly now.
monthlyTasks.04: Pot on seedlings. Established plants producing flower spikes.
monthlyTasks.05: Flower spikes emerging! Stunning bell-shaped flowers. Loved by bumblebees.
monthlyTasks.06: Peak flowering. Enjoy. Leave some to self-seed for next generation.
monthlyTasks.07: Flowering finishes. Let seed pods develop and scatter naturally. Cut back if tidy-minded.
monthlyTasks.08: Self-sown seedlings appearing. Transplant to where you want them.
monthlyTasks.09: Plant out pot-grown foxgloves to their final positions for flowering next year.
monthlyTasks.10: Young plants establishing. Water if dry.
monthlyTasks.11: Mulch around plants for winter protection.
monthlyTasks.12: Rest. Foxgloves are tough — they'll be fine.

lavender.wateringNotes: Very drought tolerant once established. Hates sitting in wet soil — this is the number one killer. Sharp drainage is essential.
lavender.feedingSchedule: None needed — lavender actually prefers poor soil

commonProblems.symptoms: Plant becomes bare and woody at base with growth only at tips
commonProblems.treatment: Prune after flowering — cut back to just above the woody growth. Never cut into old wood — it won't regrow.

commonProblems.symptoms: Grey, wilting foliage, plant dying from centre
commonProblems.treatment: Improve drainage. Add grit to planting hole. Don't overwater. Raise bed if needed.

lavender.harvestTips: Cut flower stems just as buds begin to open for drying. Hang upside down in bunches in warm, dry place. Use in sachets, baking, or just enjoy the scent.
lavender.repottingTrigger: When roots circle the bottom of the pot
lavender.repottingSize: 30cm pot minimum for mature plant. Add extra grit for drainage.

monthlyTasks.02: Plan where to plant new lavender. Order plants if buying.
monthlyTasks.03: Sow seed indoors — slow and erratic germination. Buying plants is easier. Lightly prune established plants to shape.
monthlyTasks.04: Plant out container-grown lavender. Add grit to planting hole for drainage.
monthlyTasks.05: New growth appearing. No feeding needed.
monthlyTasks.06: Flower buds forming. Bees will be queueing up soon.
monthlyTasks.07: Peak flowering! Harvest for drying. The scent is incredible. Bees absolutely love it.
monthlyTasks.08: Prune after flowering — cut back flower stalks and about 2cm of leafy growth. Shape into a dome. CRITICAL: never cut into old wood.
monthlyTasks.09: Take semi-ripe cuttings for new plants if wanted.
monthlyTasks.10: No action needed. Ensure good drainage before winter wet.
monthlyTasks.11: Protect tender varieties with fleece in harsh winters. English lavender is generally fine.

heather.wateringNotes: Use rainwater if possible — heather hates lime. Keep moist but well-drained. Ericaceous compost only.

commonProblems.symptoms: Leaves turn yellow-green, growth stunted
commonProblems.cause: Soil/compost is too alkaline, or watering with hard water
commonProblems.treatment: Use ericaceous compost. Water with rainwater. Avoid lime-containing feeds.

commonProblems.symptoms: Growth only at tips, bare woody stems
commonProblems.treatment: Trim lightly after flowering. Don't cut into old wood.

heather.harvestTips: Not typically harvested. Enjoyed for year-round colour and texture in the garden.
heather.repottingTrigger: When roots fill pot or plant looks congested
heather.repottingSize: Use ericaceous compost only. 25-30cm pot or plant in acid soil beds.

monthlyTasks.01: Check plants haven't been lifted by frost — firm back in if needed.
monthlyTasks.02: Winter-flowering Erica varieties still going strong.
monthlyTasks.03: Trim winter-flowering types after flowers fade. Light shape only.
monthlyTasks.04: Plant new heathers. Feed established plants with ericaceous fertiliser.
monthlyTasks.05: New growth appearing. Mulch with bark chips or pine needles (acidic).
monthlyTasks.06: Growing steadily. Water in dry spells — preferably with rainwater.
monthlyTasks.08: Peak flowering for summer varieties. Bees love heather.
monthlyTasks.09: Continue flowering. Trim summer-flowering types after blooms fade.
monthlyTasks.10: Autumn colour. Winter Erica varieties starting to bud up.
monthlyTasks.11: Winter-flowering heathers beginning. Tough as old boots in cold.
monthlyTasks.12: Erica carnea blooming through winter. A splash of colour when everything else is dormant.

daffodil.wateringNotes: Water during active growth if dry. Stop once foliage dies back. Bulbs rot if waterlogged in summer.
daffodil.feedingSchedule: Tomato feed after flowering while foliage is still green — this feeds next year's bulb

commonProblems.symptoms: Leaves appear but no flower buds
commonProblems.cause: Bulbs too shallow, congested clumps, foliage cut too early last year, poor feeding
commonProblems.treatment: Feed after flowering. Don't cut foliage for 6 weeks after flowering. Lift and divide congested clumps in autumn.

commonProblems.symptoms: Soft, rotten bulb with large maggot inside
commonProblems.cause: Fly lays eggs near base of dying foliage
commonProblems.treatment: Firm soil around base after flowering. Cover dying foliage with mesh.

daffodil.harvestTips: Pick flowers for vases when buds are 'goose-necked' (bent at right angle). They'll open indoors. Don't mix with other cut flowers immediately — daffodil sap is toxic to other flowers.
daffodil.repottingTrigger: Lift and divide when clumps become congested (every 3-5 years)
daffodil.repottingSize: 25-30cm pot, plant 5-7 bulbs per pot for a good display

monthlyTasks.01: Shoots appearing! If in pots, ensure they're not drying out.
monthlyTasks.02: Flower buds forming. Early varieties may already be blooming. Feed with tomato feed.
monthlyTasks.03: Peak flowering! Enjoy the show. Deadhead spent flowers but leave the stems.
monthlyTasks.04: Flowering finishes. CRITICAL: Do NOT cut foliage — let it die back naturally for 6 weeks. It's feeding next year's bulb.
monthlyTasks.05: Foliage yellowing. Continue to leave it. Stop watering pots once foliage dies.
monthlyTasks.06: Foliage died back. Rest period begins. Lift and store bulbs from pots if needed.
monthlyTasks.07: Bulbs dormant. Leave in ground or store in cool, dry place.
monthlyTasks.08: Order new bulbs for autumn planting.
monthlyTasks.09: Plant new bulbs! 2-3x depth of the bulb. Pointy end up. Earlier is better for daffodils.
monthlyTasks.10: Continue planting. Last chance for a good spring display.
monthlyTasks.11: Late planting still possible but not ideal. Roots establishing underground.
monthlyTasks.12: Rest. Bulbs are developing roots in the cold soil — the chill is essential for flowering.
